0: Effect of substituting grass-clover silage with maize silage for dairy cows on nutrient digestibility, rumen metabolism, enteric methane emission and total carbon footprint

D.N. Brask-Pedersen a M. Lamminen a, 1 , L. Mogensen b , A.L.F. Hellwing a M. Johansen P. Lund a , M. Larsen , M.R. Weisbjerg a , C.F. Borsting a, a Aarhus University, Department of Animal and Veterinary Sciences, AU Viborg, Recearch Centre Foulum, P.O. Box 50, DK-8830, Tjele, Denmark b Aarhus University, Department of Agroecology, AU Foulum, P.O. Box 50, DK-8830, Tjele, Denmark
1:
<list>Substituting grass-clover silage with maize silage for dairy cows led to. 
15% lower methane emission per kg dry matter intake. 
Only minor effects on digestibility of nutrients and no influence on rumen pH. 
A higher carbon footprint from production of grass-clover silage than maize silage, but lower when change in soil carbon is included. 
Slightly lower total carbon footprint when net carbon sequestration was included.</list>

ARTICLEINFO|ABSTRACT Keywords: Carbon footprint Maize silage Grass-clover silage Forage Enteric methane Life cycle assessment (LCA)|The aim of this experiment was to determine the optimal ratio between maize silage and grass-clover silage in lactating dairy cow diets. The evaluation was based on nutrient digestibility, rumen metabolism and total carbon footprint (CF). Carbon footprint included enteric methane emission and CF from feed production, transportation, processing, and C sequestration in the soil, based on a Life Cycle Assessment (LCA) approach. Four diets varying in proportions of grass-clover silage and maize silage (100:0, 67:33, 33:67, and 0:100 on dry matter (DM) basis, respectively) were fed ad libitum to 4 lactating Holstein cows (a fifth cow was involved later in the study) fitted with ruminal, duodenal, and ileal canulae in a 4 X 4 Latin square design. Nutrient digestibility was measured using chromium oxide and titanium oxide as flow markers, and methane and CO2 emissions were measured in open-circuit respiration chambers on 5 consecutive days for each cow in each period. Data were analyzed with treatment and period as fixed effects and cow as random effect. DM intake (DMI) increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the feed ration. Under the conditions in the present study, diets had only minor effects on digestibility of nutrients and there was no influence on rumen pH. Total VFA concentration decreased linearly with increased level of maize silage, but there were only minor effects on molar proportions of VFAs in the rumen. However, there was a linear increase in duodenal flow of microbial DM with increasing level of maize silage. The cows produced 30.7, 29.4. 28.4, and 26.2 L of methane/kg of DMI on diets 100:0, 66:33, 33:66, and 0:100, respectively (P = 0.02 for linear effect), which is equivalent to a 15% decrease, when all grass- clover silage was replaced by maize silage. Despite the significant reduction in enteric methane emission with increased proportion of maize silage, only a 4% decrease in total CF per kg DMI was found, when including CF from feed production, transportation, processing, manure, energy use and C sequestration in the soil, when using a LCA approach. If direct Land Use Change (LUC) or indirect LUC was included in estimation of CF, the reduction in CF when replacing all grass-clover silage was also 4-6%. However, the total CF was highly dependent on assumptions related to yield of grass-clover and maize.
2: Livestock contributes significantly to the total greenhouse gas (GHG) emissions from agriculture (Bellarby et al., 2013), with land use change (LUC), enteric fermentation from ruminants and manure management as the largest contributors. Enteric methane (CH4) is a natural by-product of microbial fermentation of nutrients in the digestive tract of animals (Hristov et al., 2013; Hart et al., 2015). Globally, most attention has been directed to enteric CH4 emissions from ruminants, because it constitutes a substantial share of the total non-CO2 emissions from agriculture (Bellarby et al., 2013), and is the most important contributor to the GHG emissions on dairy farms. The enteric CH4 emission per animal varies depending on the feed composition and feed quality. The emission level of CH4 has been found to vary from 2 to 12% of the gross energy (GE) intake (Johnson and Johnson, 1995) under extreme cir- cumstances. However, variations between 3 and 7% was found by Martin et al. (2010), whereas model predictions averaged 6.4% for EU cows (Niu et al., 2018), which seems to be more realistic in intensive dairy production systems.

Many studies have investigated the nutritional possibilities to reduce the enteric CH4 emission (Boadi et al., 2004; Beauchemin et al., 2008; Brask et al., 2013; Hristov et al., 2013). However, the evaluation of a single environmental criterion, such as enteric CH4, isolated from all others like carbon footprint (CF) related to feed production, may lead to misinterpretations of the overall effect on the CF of the products. For example, the use of maize silage in cattle diets has been reported to result in higher DMI and milk yield (Hristov et al., 2004) and to reduce methane emission compared to grass-clover silage (Brask et al., 2013; Hassanat et al., 2013; van Gastelen et al., 2015). However, the pro- duction of maize at the expense of grass-clover also increases the GHG emissions from fields due to increased carbon losses from the soil (Vel- linga and Hoving, 2011).

Many studies have compared feeding maize silage versus grass or grass-clover (starch VS. NDF) diets to dairy cattle, with the aim of studying the effect on rumen digestion kinetics and CH4 emission (Brask et al., 2013; Hassanat et al., 2013; van Gastelen et al., 2015). However, very few studies have addressed the effects on a system level.

Grass-clover and maize silage are used in many countries as the major sources of forage in dairy diets. How grass-clover and maize si- lages are combined in optimal diets depends on climate, yield of the crops, prices, etc., however, in the future, climate impact is very relevant to include when making decisions about the choice of forage. Under- standing the digestibility processes of these forages is important for reducing enteric methane by the optimal choice of forage.

The main objective of the current experiment was to determine the optimal ratio between maize silage and grass-clover silage in lactating dairy cow diets based on digestion, rumen metabolism, enteric CH4 emission and total CF including the whole chain from feed to milk. It was hypothesized that increasing the proportion of maize silage in the diet at the expense of grass-clover silage will decrease CH4 emission per kg DMI linearly, but when using a system approach the total CF will be only slightly affected by this substantial shift in forage source.

6: The experiment was conducted at Danish Cattle Research centre (AU Foulum, Tjele, Denmark). All experimental procedures were approved by The Animal Experiments Inspectorate, Ministry of Environment and Food of Denmark in accordance with the guidelines established by the European Union Directive 2010/63/EU and the current Danish legisla- tion on animal experimentation (Law No. 474, May 14, 2014).

Four rumen and intestinally cannulated Holstein cows were used, and at the start of the experiment, the four COWS were on average ( SD) 363 423 days in milk (DIM) and produced 25.9 I 5.26 kg milk per day.

One of the 4 cows was in very late lactation (997 DIM) compared to the others (on average 152 DIM). This was considered justified as the milk yield of the late-lactation cow at the start of the trial was stable and on an acceptable level compared to that of the other 3 cows (on average 28.3 kg/d). However, during the experiment, the milk yield of the late-lactation cow started to decline rapidly, but without an equiv- alent decrease in DMI, and a fifth Holstein cow (34 DIM, milk yield 42.4 kg/d in the beginning of period 3) was added to the experiment in the last two periods. The milk yield data of the low yielding cow was omitted from the experiment due to large deviation from other cows (e.g., 4.2 VS. 33.3 kg/d in period 3), resulting in n = 14 for milk parameters and n = 18 for other parameters. The body weight (BW) of the late-lactation cow could not be determined reliably because the weight exceeded the limit of the scale (1000 kg). The BW of the other three cows was on average 514 I 60.3 kg at the beginning of the experiment, and 557 + 56.1 kg at the end of the experiment. The BW of the fifth cow was 669 kg at the beginning of period 3, and 673 kg at the end of the experiment.

All cows were fitted with ruminal cannulas (#1C, Bar Diamond Inc., Parma, ID, USA), duodenal cannulas (open T-piece placed 60 cm caudal to pylorus) and ileal cannulas (open T-piece placed 20 cm cranial to caecum) and were used in a balanced 4 X 4 Latin square experiment with four 21-d periods. Every period in the experiment started day 0 at 1700 h and ended day 21 at 1700 h, and therefore, day 21 and 0 in two adjacent periods was the same calendar day. Cows were randomly allocated to one of the following four experimental treatments where the forage part had varying proportions of grass-clover silage and maize silage (100:0, 67:33, 33:67, and 0:100 on DM basis, respectively). Grass- clover silage and maize silage (cv. Ambition) were ensiled in bunker silos without the use of ensiling additives. Grass-clover silage was preserved from the spring growth of a crop mixture consisting of perennial ryegrass, hybrid ryegrass, white clover, and red clover (60%, 23%, 9% and 8% (weight basis) in the seed mixture, respectively).

Feeds were offered to cows as total mixed rations (TMR) having a concentrate-to-forage ratio of 30:70 in DM and consisting of experi- mental silages, rolled wheat, rapeseed meal, soybean meal, lipid sup- plement, minerals and vitamins (Table Lower crude protein (CP) and higher starch concentration of maize silage than that of grass-clover silage (Table 2) was partly compensated by changing the proportion of rapeseed meal and wheat in the diets. Isoenergetic diets were formulated according to the Nordic Feed Evaluation System (NorFor (Volden, 2011)).

The TMRs were mixed daily, and cows had ad libitum access to TMR and water throughout the experiment. Cows were housed individually in a tie stalls and milked twice daily at 0630 and 1645 h. TMR was fed to cows twice daily at 0700 (approximately 40% of ad libitum intake) and 1730 h (approximately 60% of ad libitum intake), and orts were removed once daily at 1720 h. The orts were kept at 2 to 3 kg/d on fresh matter basis in order to minimize feed selection.

10: <header>2.2. Measurements, sampling and chemical analysis </header>

Feed intake and milk yield were recorded daily throughout the experiment, but only measurements on d 12-16 and d 16-21 in each period were used for statistical analysis. Representative samples of feed ingredients were collected weekly, and those of TMR daily during d 12-21. The samples of TMR were combined by period to provide a composite sample for chemical analyses, and stored at -20°C until ana- lyses. collection procedure was arranged to give representative samples of the diurnal flow (i.e., every second hour of the 24-h day). Samples from duodenum and ileum were collected in tube-formed plastic bags attached to the cannula with a plastic knee. Spot samples of feces were collected in cups. Duodenal and ileal digesta, and feces were stored at -20 °C and all were pooled within cow and period. Ruminal liquid was sampled from the ventral ruminal sac using a collection tube (#RT, Bar Diamond Inc., Parma, ID, USA). The pH of rumen liquid was determined immediately, and samples (8 mL) were taken for later determination of volatile fatty acids (VFA) and NH3 and stored at -20°C until analysis. Feed, duodenal and ileal digesta and feces were all frozen (-20 °C), freeze-dried, and ground using a hammer mill through a 1-mm screen before chemical analysis.

To isolate ruminal microbes, rumen liquid was collected on d 21 in each period. The rumen liquid was collected with a cup in the ventral part of the ruminal sac and then filtered through 2 layers of cheesecloth,

and transferred to prewarmed vacuum-insulated bottles. Samples were taken directly to the laboratory and handled according to Brask et al. (2015). In brief, feed particles and protozoa were removed by double centrifugation at 500 X g for 5 min at 3 °C. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Then, the pellet was collected and resuspended in 200 mL of NaCl (0.9%) and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Next, the pellet of microbial matter was stored at -20 °C. Finally, the microbial matter was freeze-dried and analyzed for ash, nitrogen, and purine content.

The ingredients of TMR and TMR samples were analyzed for DM, ash, CP, NDF, ADL, starch (not from grass-clover silage), sugar, crude fat, and in vitro OM digestibility. Dry matter content of both forages and concentrates was determined by drying at 60 °C for 48 h. Ash content was determined by combustion performed at 525 °C for 6 h. Nitrogen was determined using the Dumas principle (Hansen, 1989) using a Vario MAX CN (Elementar Analysesysteme GmbH), and the CP was calculated
Table 2
Chemical composition of the experimental feedstuffs (mean+SD).

1 In DM: 10.4% of lactic acid, 2.70% of acetic acid, 0.002% of propionic acid, butyric acid not detected, 7.15% of N is NH3-N, pH 3.99. 2 In DM: 8.22% of lactic acid, 1.79% of acetic acid, 0.019% of propionic acid, butyric acid not detected, 8.87% of N is NH3-N, pH 3.68. 3 Includes rapeseed meal (33.3% of DM), soybean meal (41.7% of DM), minerals (Vm 3 Gron, Vilofoss, Fredericia, Denmark; 9.17% of DM), sodium bicarbonate (7.92% of DM), lipid supplement (Lipitec Bovi 85, Ringe, Denmark; 6.25% of DM), and A, D and E vitamin premix (Suplex ADE/Selen Gron, Vilofoss; 1.67% of DM). 4 Number of samples analysed. 5 In vivo organic matter digestibility calculated as 4.10 + 0.959 X in vitro OM digestibility (%) for silages (Volden 2011) and as 5.38 + 0.867 X enzymatic di- gestibility of OM (%) for concentrates (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993).

as N X 6.25. Crude fat was analyzed as Soxhlet extraction with petro- leum ether (Soxtec 2050, Foss Analytical, Hillerod, Denmark) after hy- drolyzing with HCl (Stoldt, 1952). Starch content in feed, digesta, and feces was determined enzymatically (Kristensen et al., 2007). Total sugar concentration was determined by the Luff-Schoorl method (Schoorl, 1929) described in 71/250/EEC. The NDF concentration was analyzed using neutral detergent extraction according to Mertens (2002) with a Fibertec M6 System (Foss Analytical) using heat-stable amylase and sodium sulfite, and corrected for residual ash. The iNDF in freeze dried ground feed samples was determined as residual NDF after 288 h (12 d) of Dacron bag (12-um pore size; Saatitech S.p.A., 22,070 Veniano, Como, Italy) incubation in the rumen of 3 non-lactating heifers fed a standard ration (Akerlind et al., 2011). Concentrations of VFA in rumen fluid were analyzed by gas chromatography as described by Kristensen et al. (1996). Glucose and L-lactate were determined with membrane-immobilized substrate-specific oxidases using a YSI 2900D Biochemistry Analyzer (YSI Inc., Yellow Springs, OH). Ammonia in ruminal samples were determined after 1:20 dilution with 100 mM phosphate buffer by using an enzymatic assay (AM 1015, Randox Lab- oratories Ltd., Crumlin, UK) adapted for use on an autoanalyzer.

Chromium oxide was determined by spectrophotometry after oxidation to chromate (Schürch et al., 1950). Titanium oxide was analyzed according to Myers et al. (2004) with the exception that 15 mL of 30% H2O2 was added instead of 10 mL, and 5 drops of 30% H2O2 were added to the solution just before measuring the absorbance. In vitro digestibility of OM in forage and TMR was determined by anaerobic incubation in diluted rumen fluid, followed by incubation of the insol- uble material with pepsin HCI solution (Tilley and Terry, 1963). In concentrates, determination of in vitro digestibility of OM was made by treating the samples with pepsin-HCl solution and subsequent incuba- tion with enzymes (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993). Protein, fat, and lactose (monohydrate) concentrations in milk were measured by an infrared analyzer (Milkoscan FT, Foss Analytical) at Eurofins Steins Laboratories (Holstebro, Denmark).

Methane and CO2 emission were measured on d 16-21 for each cow. The cows were moved to the respiration chambers at 1700 h on d 16. The four 17m³ open-circuit respiration chambers were constructed of transparent polycarbonate and placed in a square two by two, to pro- mote visual contact between cows. The chambers were located in the same barn as where cows were housed outside of chamber period in order to minimize environmental changes. For a more detailed description of the chambers and measuring equipment see Hellwing et al. (2012). Chambers were opened for milking, cleaning and feeding twice daily. One chamber was finished before the next one was opened, and it took approximately 15 min to finish each chamber. Starting time for the work in the chambers was the same as milking times. Cows were always handled in the same order in the chamber. After the chambers had been opened in connection with milking and feeding, the feed- troughs were covered with a lid, which was automatically removed half an hour later. This procedure was applied to ensure that the air composition in the chamber was stabile before the cow was allowed to eat. Data points in connection with opening of the chambers were deleted from the dataset.

The inlet air from the chambers was drawn from the barn and to minimize the impact of background differences between chambers, cows switched chambers diagonally after the first 3 days of measurement. Cow and chamber were confounded throughout the experiment.

In period 2, CH4 and CO2 emission were only measured on d 16-19 due to a technical error of an instrument. On d 20-21 in period 3 and 4 the fifth cow was included in the CH4 and CO2 measurements at the expense of the very low yielding late-lactation cow.

The daily CH4 and CO2 emission were calculated as CH4 or CO2 produced during the complete measuring period divided by measuring length in minutes (excluding periods without measurements) multiplied by 1440 min (See more details in Hellwing et al., 2012). The CH4 and CO2 emission are given in L/day under standard temperature and

pressure (0 °C, 101.325 kPa). Recovery tests were performed as described by Mesgaran et al. (2020) both before, during, and after the experiment. The measured values were corrected with the average re- covery obtained per chamber and gas. In total 11 CO2 recoveries were performed per chamber and the average recoveries for chamber 1, 2, 3 and 4 were 99.8 + 1.1%, 100.1 1 1.7%, 99.6 + 1.2%, and 100.2 + 0.9%, respectively. For CH4, there were performed 5, 5, 6, and 5 re- covery tests in chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, and the average recovery for chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4 were 99.0 + 0.8%, 100.0 + 1.4%, 99.5 + 0.8%, and 99.6 I 0. 8%, respectively.

Glucose and L-lactate were determined with membrane-immobilized substrate-specific oxidases using a YSI 2900D Biochemistry Analyzer (YSI Inc., Yellow Springs, OH). Ammonia in ruminal samples were determined after 1:20 dilution with 100 mM phosphate buffer by using an enzymatic assay (AM 1015, Randox Lab- oratories Ltd., Crumlin, UK) adapted for use on an autoanalyzer.

Chromium oxide was determined by spectrophotometry after oxidation to chromate (Schürch et al., 1950). Titanium oxide was analyzed according to Myers et al. (2004) with the exception that 15 mL of 30% H2O2 was added instead of 10 mL, and 5 drops of 30% H2O2 were added to the solution just before measuring the absorbance. In vitro digestibility of OM in forage and TMR was determined by anaerobic incubation in diluted rumen fluid, followed by incubation of the insol- uble material with pepsin HCI solution (Tilley and Terry, 1963). In concentrates, determination of in vitro digestibility of OM was made by treating the samples with pepsin-HCl solution and subsequent incuba- tion with enzymes (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993). Protein, fat, and lactose (monohydrate) concentrations in milk were measured by an infrared analyzer (Milkoscan FT, Foss Analytical) at Eurofins Steins Laboratories (Holstebro, Denmark).

Methane and CO2 emission were measured on d 16-21 for each cow. The cows were moved to the respiration chambers at 1700 h on d 16. The four 17m³ open-circuit respiration chambers were constructed of transparent polycarbonate and placed in a square two by two, to pro- mote visual contact between cows. The chambers were located in the same barn as where cows were housed outside of chamber period in order to minimize environmental changes. For a more detailed description of the chambers and measuring equipment see Hellwing et al. (2012). Chambers were opened for milking, cleaning and feeding twice daily. One chamber was finished before the next one was opened, and it took approximately 15 min to finish each chamber. Starting time for the work in the chambers was the same as milking times. Cows were always handled in the same order in the chamber. After the chambers had been opened in connection with milking and feeding, the feed- troughs were covered with a lid, which was automatically removed half an hour later. This procedure was applied to ensure that the air composition in the chamber was stabile before the cow was allowed to eat. Data points in connection with opening of the chambers were deleted from the dataset.

The inlet air from the chambers was drawn from the barn and to minimize the impact of background differences between chambers, cows switched chambers diagonally after the first 3 days of measurement. Cow and chamber were confounded throughout the experiment.

In period 2, CH4 and CO2 emission were only measured on d 16-19 due to a technical error of an instrument. On d 20-21 in period 3 and 4 the fifth cow was included in the CH4 and CO2 measurements at the expense of the very low yielding late-lactation cow.

The daily CH4 and CO2 emission were calculated as CH4 or CO2 produced during the complete measuring period divided by measuring length in minutes (excluding periods without measurements) multiplied by 1440 min (See more details in Hellwing et al., 2012). The CH4 and CO2 emission are given in L/day under standard temperature and

pressure (0 °C, 101.325 kPa). Recovery tests were performed as described by Mesgaran et al. (2020) both before, during, and after the experiment. The measured values were corrected with the average re- covery obtained per chamber and gas. In total 11 CO2 recoveries were performed per chamber and the average recoveries for chamber 1, 2, 3 and 4 were 99.8 + 1.1%, 100.1 1 1.7%, 99.6 + 1.2%, and 100.2 + 0.9%, respectively. For CH4, there were performed 5, 5, 6, and 5 re- covery tests in chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, and the average recovery for chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4 were 99.0 + 0.8%, 100.0 + 1.4%, 99.5 + 0.8%, and 99.6 I 0. 8%, respectively.
13: <header>2.2. Measurements, sampling and chemical analysis </header>

Feed intake and milk yield were recorded daily throughout the experiment, but only measurements on d 12-16 and d 16-21 in each period were used for statistical analysis. Representative samples of feed ingredients were collected weekly, and those of TMR daily during d 12-21. The samples of TMR were combined by period to provide a composite sample for chemical analyses, and stored at -20°C until ana- lyses. The orts were weighed daily, and samples were collected on d 12-21 for DM determination (60 °C, 48 h). Cows were weighed in the beginning of the experiment and at the end of each period.

Chromic oxide (Cr2O3; 20 g/d) and titanium oxide (TiO2; 26 g/d) were used as intestinal digesta flow markers and administed daily directly into the rumen in two equal portions via the ruminal cannula on d 0-16. After an adaptation period of 12 d, 12 samples of ruminal fluid, duodenal and ileal contents, and feces were obtained within 4 consec- utive days covering every second hour of the day and night. The

collection procedure was arranged to give representative samples of the diurnal flow (i.e., every second hour of the 24-h day). Samples from duodenum and ileum were collected in tube-formed plastic bags attached to the cannula with a plastic knee. Spot samples of feces were collected in cups. Duodenal and ileal digesta, and feces were stored at -20 °C and all were pooled within cow and period. Ruminal liquid was sampled from the ventral ruminal sac using a collection tube (#RT, Bar Diamond Inc., Parma, ID, USA). The pH of rumen liquid was determined immediately, and samples (8 mL) were taken for later determination of volatile fatty acids (VFA) and NH3 and stored at -20°C until analysis. Feed, duodenal and ileal digesta and feces were all frozen (-20 °C), freeze-dried, and ground using a hammer mill through a 1-mm screen before chemical analysis.

To isolate ruminal microbes, rumen liquid was collected on d 21 in each period. The rumen liquid was collected with a cup in the ventral part of the ruminal sac and then filtered through 2 layers of cheesecloth,

and transferred to prewarmed vacuum-insulated bottles. Samples were taken directly to the laboratory and handled according to Brask et al. (2015). In brief, feed particles and protozoa were removed by double centrifugation at 500 X g for 5 min at 3 °C. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Then, the pellet was collected and resuspended in 200 mL of NaCl (0.9%) and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Next, the pellet of microbial matter was stored at -20 °C. Finally, the microbial matter was freeze-dried and analyzed for ash, nitrogen, and purine content.

The ingredients of TMR and TMR samples were analyzed for DM, ash, CP, NDF, ADL, starch (not from grass-clover silage), sugar, crude fat, and in vitro OM digestibility. Dry matter content of both forages and concentrates was determined by drying at 60 °C for 48 h. Ash content was determined by combustion performed at 525 °C for 6 h. Nitrogen was determined using the Dumas principle (Hansen, 1989) using a Vario MAX CN (Elementar Analysesysteme GmbH), and the CP was calculated

collection procedure was arranged to give representative samples of the diurnal flow (i.e., every second hour of the 24-h day). Samples from duodenum and ileum were collected in tube-formed plastic bags attached to the cannula with a plastic knee. Spot samples of feces were collected in cups. Duodenal and ileal digesta, and feces were stored at -20 °C and all were pooled within cow and period. Ruminal liquid was sampled from the ventral ruminal sac using a collection tube (#RT, Bar Diamond Inc., Parma, ID, USA). The pH of rumen liquid was determined immediately, and samples (8 mL) were taken for later determination of volatile fatty acids (VFA) and NH3 and stored at -20°C until analysis. Feed, duodenal and ileal digesta and feces were all frozen (-20 °C), freeze-dried, and ground using a hammer mill through a 1-mm screen before chemical analysis.

To isolate ruminal microbes, rumen liquid was collected on d 21 in each period. The rumen liquid was collected with a cup in the ventral part of the ruminal sac and then filtered through 2 layers of cheesecloth,

and transferred to prewarmed vacuum-insulated bottles. Samples were taken directly to the laboratory and handled according to Brask et al. (2015). In brief, feed particles and protozoa were removed by double centrifugation at 500 X g for 5 min at 3 °C. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Then, the pellet was collected and resuspended in 200 mL of NaCl (0.9%) and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Next, the pellet of microbial matter was stored at -20 °C. Finally, the microbial matter was freeze-dried and analyzed for ash, nitrogen, and purine content.

The ingredients of TMR and TMR samples were analyzed for DM, ash, CP, NDF, ADL, starch (not from grass-clover silage), sugar, crude fat, and in vitro OM digestibility. Dry matter content of both forages and concentrates was determined by drying at 60 °C for 48 h. Ash content was determined by combustion performed at 525 °C for 6 h. Nitrogen was determined using the Dumas principle (Hansen, 1989) using a Vario MAX CN (Elementar Analysesysteme GmbH), and the CP was calculated
Table 2
Chemical composition of the experimental feedstuffs (mean+SD).

1 In DM: 10.4% of lactic acid, 2.70% of acetic acid, 0.002% of propionic acid, butyric acid not detected, 7.15% of N is NH3-N, pH 3.99. 2 In DM: 8.22% of lactic acid, 1.79% of acetic acid, 0.019% of propionic acid, butyric acid not detected, 8.87% of N is NH3-N, pH 3.68. 3 Includes rapeseed meal (33.3% of DM), soybean meal (41.7% of DM), minerals (Vm 3 Gron, Vilofoss, Fredericia, Denmark; 9.17% of DM), sodium bicarbonate (7.92% of DM), lipid supplement (Lipitec Bovi 85, Ringe, Denmark; 6.25% of DM), and A, D and E vitamin premix (Suplex ADE/Selen Gron, Vilofoss; 1.67% of DM). 4 Number of samples analysed. 5 In vivo organic matter digestibility calculated as 4.10 + 0.959 X in vitro OM digestibility (%) for silages (Volden 2011) and as 5.38 + 0.867 X enzymatic di- gestibility of OM (%) for concentrates (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993).

as N X 6.25. Crude fat was analyzed as Soxhlet extraction with petro- leum ether (Soxtec 2050, Foss Analytical, Hillerod, Denmark) after hy- drolyzing with HCl (Stoldt, 1952). Starch content in feed, digesta, and feces was determined enzymatically (Kristensen et al., 2007). Total sugar concentration was determined by the Luff-Schoorl method (Schoorl, 1929) described in 71/250/EEC. The NDF concentration was analyzed using neutral detergent extraction according to Mertens (2002) with a Fibertec M6 System (Foss Analytical) using heat-stable amylase and sodium sulfite, and corrected for residual ash. The iNDF in freeze dried ground feed samples was determined as residual NDF after 288 h (12 d) of Dacron bag (12-um pore size; Saatitech S.p.A., 22,070 Veniano, Como, Italy) incubation in the rumen of 3 non-lactating heifers fed a standard ration (Akerlind et al., 2011). Concentrations of VFA in rumen fluid were analyzed by gas chromatography as described by Kristensen et al. (1996). Glucose and L-lactate were determined with membrane-immobilized substrate-specific oxidases using a YSI 2900D Biochemistry Analyzer (YSI Inc., Yellow Springs, OH). Ammonia in ruminal samples were determined after 1:20 dilution with 100 mM phosphate buffer by using an enzymatic assay (AM 1015, Randox Lab- oratories Ltd., Crumlin, UK) adapted for use on an autoanalyzer.

Chromium oxide was determined by spectrophotometry after oxidation to chromate (Schürch et al., 1950). Titanium oxide was analyzed according to Myers et al. (2004) with the exception that 15 mL of 30% H2O2 was added instead of 10 mL, and 5 drops of 30% H2O2 were added to the solution just before measuring the absorbance. In vitro digestibility of OM in forage and TMR was determined by anaerobic incubation in diluted rumen fluid, followed by incubation of the insol- uble material with pepsin HCI solution (Tilley and Terry, 1963). In concentrates, determination of in vitro digestibility of OM was made by treating the samples with pepsin-HCl solution and subsequent incuba- tion with enzymes (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993). Protein, fat, and lactose (monohydrate) concentrations in milk were measured by an infrared analyzer (Milkoscan FT, Foss Analytical) at Eurofins Steins Laboratories (Holstebro, Denmark).

Methane and CO2 emission were measured on d 16-21 for each cow. The cows were moved to the respiration chambers at 1700 h on d 16. The four 17m³ open-circuit respiration chambers were constructed of transparent polycarbonate and placed in a square two by two, to pro- mote visual contact between cows. The chambers were located in the same barn as where cows were housed outside of chamber period in order to minimize environmental changes. For a more detailed description of the chambers and measuring equipment see Hellwing et al. (2012). Chambers were opened for milking, cleaning and feeding twice daily. One chamber was finished before the next one was opened, and it took approximately 15 min to finish each chamber. Starting time for the work in the chambers was the same as milking times. Cows were always handled in the same order in the chamber. After the chambers had been opened in connection with milking and feeding, the feed- troughs were covered with a lid, which was automatically removed half an hour later. This procedure was applied to ensure that the air composition in the chamber was stabile before the cow was allowed to eat. Data points in connection with opening of the chambers were deleted from the dataset.

The inlet air from the chambers was drawn from the barn and to minimize the impact of background differences between chambers, cows switched chambers diagonally after the first 3 days of measurement. Cow and chamber were confounded throughout the experiment.

In period 2, CH4 and CO2 emission were only measured on d 16-19 due to a technical error of an instrument. On d 20-21 in period 3 and 4 the fifth cow was included in the CH4 and CO2 measurements at the expense of the very low yielding late-lactation cow.

The daily CH4 and CO2 emission were calculated as CH4 or CO2 produced during the complete measuring period divided by measuring length in minutes (excluding periods without measurements) multiplied by 1440 min (See more details in Hellwing et al., 2012). The CH4 and CO2 emission are given in L/day under standard temperature and

pressure (0 °C, 101.325 kPa). Recovery tests were performed as described by Mesgaran et al. (2020) both before, during, and after the experiment. The measured values were corrected with the average re- covery obtained per chamber and gas. In total 11 CO2 recoveries were performed per chamber and the average recoveries for chamber 1, 2, 3 and 4 were 99.8 + 1.1%, 100.1 1 1.7%, 99.6 + 1.2%, and 100.2 + 0.9%, respectively. For CH4, there were performed 5, 5, 6, and 5 re- covery tests in chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, and the average recovery for chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4 were 99.0 + 0.8%, 100.0 + 1.4%, 99.5 + 0.8%, and 99.6 I 0. 8%, respectively.
16: <header>2.2. Measurements, sampling and chemical analysis </header>

Feed intake and milk yield were recorded daily throughout the experiment, but only measurements on d 12-16 and d 16-21 in each period were used for statistical analysis. Representative samples of feed ingredients were collected weekly, and those of TMR daily during d 12-21. The samples of TMR were combined by period to provide a composite sample for chemical analyses, and stored at -20°C until ana- lyses. The orts were weighed daily, and samples were collected on d 12-21 for DM determination (60 °C, 48 h). Cows were weighed in the beginning of the experiment and at the end of each period.

Chromic oxide (Cr2O3; 20 g/d) and titanium oxide (TiO2; 26 g/d) were used as intestinal digesta flow markers and administed daily directly into the rumen in two equal portions via the ruminal cannula on d 0-16. After an adaptation period of 12 d, 12 samples of ruminal fluid, duodenal and ileal contents, and feces were obtained within 4 consec- utive days covering every second hour of the day and night. The

In concentrates, determination of in vitro digestibility of OM was made by treating the samples with pepsin-HCl solution and subsequent incuba- tion with enzymes (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993). Protein, fat, and lactose (monohydrate) concentrations in milk were measured by an infrared analyzer (Milkoscan FT, Foss Analytical) at Eurofins Steins Laboratories (Holstebro, Denmark).

Methane and CO2 emission were measured on d 16-21 for each cow. The cows were moved to the respiration chambers at 1700 h on d 16. The four 17m³ open-circuit respiration chambers were constructed of transparent polycarbonate and placed in a square two by two, to pro- mote visual contact between cows. The chambers were located in the same barn as where cows were housed outside of chamber period in order to minimize environmental changes. For a more detailed description of the chambers and measuring equipment see Hellwing et al. (2012). Chambers were opened for milking, cleaning and feeding twice daily. One chamber was finished before the next one was opened, and it took approximately 15 min to finish each chamber. Starting time for the work in the chambers was the same as milking times. Cows were always handled in the same order in the chamber. After the chambers had been opened in connection with milking and feeding, the feed- troughs were covered with a lid, which was automatically removed half an hour later. This procedure was applied to ensure that the air composition in the chamber was stabile before the cow was allowed to eat. Data points in connection with opening of the chambers were deleted from the dataset.

The inlet air from the chambers was drawn from the barn and to minimize the impact of background differences between chambers, cows switched chambers diagonally after the first 3 days of measurement. Cow and chamber were confounded throughout the experiment.

In period 2, CH4 and CO2 emission were only measured on d 16-19 due to a technical error of an instrument. On d 20-21 in period 3 and 4 the fifth cow was included in the CH4 and CO2 measurements at the expense of the very low yielding late-lactation cow.

The daily CH4 and CO2 emission were calculated as CH4 or CO2 produced during the complete measuring period divided by measuring length in minutes (excluding periods without measurements) multiplied by 1440 min (See more details in Hellwing et al., 2012). The CH4 and CO2 emission are given in L/day under standard temperature and

pressure (0 °C, 101.325 kPa). Recovery tests were performed as described by Mesgaran et al. (2020) both before, during, and after the experiment. The measured values were corrected with the average re- covery obtained per chamber and gas. In total 11 CO2 recoveries were performed per chamber and the average recoveries for chamber 1, 2, 3 and 4 were 99.8 + 1.1%, 100.1 1 1.7%, 99.6 + 1.2%, and 100.2 + 0.9%, respectively. For CH4, there were performed 5, 5, 6, and 5 re- covery tests in chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, and the average recovery for chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4 were 99.0 + 0.8%, 100.0 + 1.4%, 99.5 + 0.8%, and 99.6 I 0. 8%, respectively.
18: <header>2.2. Measurements, sampling and chemical analysis </header>

Feed intake and milk yield were recorded daily throughout the experiment, but only measurements on d 12-16 and d 16-21 in each period were used for statistical analysis. Representative samples of feed ingredients were collected weekly, and those of TMR daily during d 12-21. The samples of TMR were combined by period to provide a composite sample for chemical analyses, and stored at -20°C until ana- lyses. The orts were weighed daily, and samples were collected on d 12-21 for DM determination (60 °C, 48 h). Cows were weighed in the beginning of the experiment and at the end of each period.

Chromic oxide (Cr2O3; 20 g/d) and titanium oxide (TiO2; 26 g/d) were used as intestinal digesta flow markers and administed daily directly into the rumen in two equal portions via the ruminal cannula on d 0-16. After an adaptation period of 12 d, 12 samples of ruminal fluid, duodenal and ileal contents, and feces were obtained within 4 consec- utive days covering every second hour of the day and night. The

collection procedure was arranged to give representative samples of the diurnal flow (i.e., every second hour of the 24-h day). Samples from duodenum and ileum were collected in tube-formed plastic bags attached to the cannula with a plastic knee. Spot samples of feces were collected in cups. Duodenal and ileal digesta, and feces were stored at -20 °C and all were pooled within cow and period. Ruminal liquid was sampled from the ventral ruminal sac using a collection tube (#RT, Bar Diamond Inc., Parma, ID, USA). The pH of rumen liquid was determined immediately, and samples (8 mL) were taken for later determination of volatile fatty acids (VFA) and NH3 and stored at -20°C until analysis. Feed, duodenal and ileal digesta and feces were all frozen (-20 °C), freeze-dried, and ground using a hammer mill through a 1-mm screen before chemical analysis.

To isolate ruminal microbes, rumen liquid was collected on d 21 in each period. The rumen liquid was collected with a cup in the ventral part of the ruminal sac and then filtered through 2 layers of cheesecloth,

and transferred to prewarmed vacuum-insulated bottles. Samples were taken directly to the laboratory and handled according to Brask et al. (2015). In brief, feed particles and protozoa were removed by double centrifugation at 500 X g for 5 min at 3 °C. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Then, the pellet was collected and resuspended in 200 mL of NaCl (0.9%) and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Next, the pellet of microbial matter was stored at -20 °C. Finally, the microbial matter was freeze-dried and analyzed for ash, nitrogen, and purine content.

The ingredients of TMR and TMR samples were analyzed for DM, ash, CP, NDF, ADL, starch (not from grass-clover silage), sugar, crude fat, and in vitro OM digestibility. Dry matter content of both forages and concentrates was determined by drying at 60 °C for 48 h. Ash content was determined by combustion performed at 525 °C for 6 h. Nitrogen was determined using the Dumas principle (Hansen, 1989) using a Vario MAX CN (Elementar Analysesysteme GmbH), and the CP was calculated

To isolate ruminal microbes, rumen liquid was collected on d 21 in each period. The rumen liquid was collected with a cup in the ventral part of the ruminal sac and then filtered through 2 layers of cheesecloth,

and transferred to prewarmed vacuum-insulated bottles. Samples were taken directly to the laboratory and handled according to Brask et al. (2015). In brief, feed particles and protozoa were removed by double centrifugation at 500 X g for 5 min at 3 °C. The supernatant was collected and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Then, the pellet was collected and resuspended in 200 mL of NaCl (0.9%) and centrifuged at 17,300 X g for 20 min at 3 °C. Next, the pellet of microbial matter was stored at -20 °C. Finally, the microbial matter was freeze-dried and analyzed for ash, nitrogen, and purine content.

The ingredients of TMR and TMR samples were analyzed for DM, ash, CP, NDF, ADL, starch (not from grass-clover silage), sugar, crude fat, and in vitro OM digestibility. Dry matter content of both forages and concentrates was determined by drying at 60 °C for 48 h. Ash content was determined by combustion performed at 525 °C for 6 h. Nitrogen was determined using the Dumas principle (Hansen, 1989) using a Vario MAX CN (Elementar Analysesysteme GmbH), and the CP was calculated
Table 2
Chemical composition of the experimental feedstuffs (mean+SD).

1 In DM: 10.4% of lactic acid, 2.70% of acetic acid, 0.002% of propionic acid, butyric acid not detected, 7.15% of N is NH3-N, pH 3.99. 2 In DM: 8.22% of lactic acid, 1.79% of acetic acid, 0.019% of propionic acid, butyric acid not detected, 8.87% of N is NH3-N, pH 3.68. 3 Includes rapeseed meal (33.3% of DM), soybean meal (41.7% of DM), minerals (Vm 3 Gron, Vilofoss, Fredericia, Denmark; 9.17% of DM), sodium bicarbonate (7.92% of DM), lipid supplement (Lipitec Bovi 85, Ringe, Denmark; 6.25% of DM), and A, D and E vitamin premix (Suplex ADE/Selen Gron, Vilofoss; 1.67% of DM). 4 Number of samples analysed. 5 In vivo organic matter digestibility calculated as 4.10 + 0.959 X in vitro OM digestibility (%) for silages (Volden 2011) and as 5.38 + 0.867 X enzymatic di- gestibility of OM (%) for concentrates (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993).

as N X 6.25. Crude fat was analyzed as Soxhlet extraction with petro- leum ether (Soxtec 2050, Foss Analytical, Hillerod, Denmark) after hy- drolyzing with HCl (Stoldt, 1952). Starch content in feed, digesta, and feces was determined enzymatically (Kristensen et al., 2007). Total sugar concentration was determined by the Luff-Schoorl method (Schoorl, 1929) described in 71/250/EEC. The NDF concentration was analyzed using neutral detergent extraction according to Mertens (2002) with a Fibertec M6 System (Foss Analytical) using heat-stable amylase and sodium sulfite, and corrected for residual ash. The iNDF in freeze dried ground feed samples was determined as residual NDF after 288 h (12 d) of Dacron bag (12-um pore size; Saatitech S.p.A., 22,070 Veniano, Como, Italy) incubation in the rumen of 3 non-lactating heifers fed a standard ration (Akerlind et al., 2011). Concentrations of VFA in rumen fluid were analyzed by gas chromatography as described by Kristensen et al. (1996). Glucose and L-lactate were determined with membrane-immobilized substrate-specific oxidases using a YSI 2900D Biochemistry Analyzer (YSI Inc., Yellow Springs, OH). Ammonia in ruminal samples were determined after 1:20 dilution with 100 mM phosphate buffer by using an enzymatic assay (AM 1015, Randox Lab- oratories Ltd., Crumlin, UK) adapted for use on an autoanalyzer.

Chromium oxide was determined by spectrophotometry after oxidation to chromate (Schürch et al., 1950). Titanium oxide was analyzed according to Myers et al. (2004) with the exception that 15 mL of 30% H2O2 was added instead of 10 mL, and 5 drops of 30% H2O2 were added to the solution just before measuring the absorbance. In vitro digestibility of OM in forage and TMR was determined by anaerobic incubation in diluted rumen fluid, followed by incubation of the insol- uble material with pepsin HCI solution (Tilley and Terry, 1963). In concentrates, determination of in vitro digestibility of OM was made by treating the samples with pepsin-HCl solution and subsequent incuba- tion with enzymes (Weisbjerg and Hvelplund, 1993). Protein, fat, and lactose (monohydrate) concentrations in milk were measured by an infrared analyzer (Milkoscan FT, Foss Analytical) at Eurofins Steins Laboratories (Holstebro, Denmark).

Methane and CO2 emission were measured on d 16-21 for each cow. The cows were moved to the respiration chambers at 1700 h on d 16. The four 17m³ open-circuit respiration chambers were constructed of transparent polycarbonate and placed in a square two by two, to pro- mote visual contact between cows. The chambers were located in the same barn as where cows were housed outside of chamber period in order to minimize environmental changes. For a more detailed description of the chambers and measuring equipment see Hellwing et al. (2012). Chambers were opened for milking, cleaning and feeding twice daily. One chamber was finished before the next one was opened, and it took approximately 15 min to finish each chamber. Starting time for the work in the chambers was the same as milking times. Cows were always handled in the same order in the chamber. After the chambers had been opened in connection with milking and feeding, the feed- troughs were covered with a lid, which was automatically removed half an hour later. This procedure was applied to ensure that the air composition in the chamber was stabile before the cow was allowed to eat. Data points in connection with opening of the chambers were deleted from the dataset.

The inlet air from the chambers was drawn from the barn and to minimize the impact of background differences between chambers, cows switched chambers diagonally after the first 3 days of measurement. Cow and chamber were confounded throughout the experiment.

In period 2, CH4 and CO2 emission were only measured on d 16-19 due to a technical error of an instrument. On d 20-21 in period 3 and 4 the fifth cow was included in the CH4 and CO2 measurements at the expense of the very low yielding late-lactation cow.

The daily CH4 and CO2 emission were calculated as CH4 or CO2 produced during the complete measuring period divided by measuring length in minutes (excluding periods without measurements) multiplied by 1440 min (See more details in Hellwing et al., 2012). The CH4 and CO2 emission are given in L/day under standard temperature and

pressure (0 °C, 101.325 kPa). Recovery tests were performed as described by Mesgaran et al. (2020) both before, during, and after the experiment. The measured values were corrected with the average re- covery obtained per chamber and gas. In total 11 CO2 recoveries were performed per chamber and the average recoveries for chamber 1, 2, 3 and 4 were 99.8 + 1.1%, 100.1 1 1.7%, 99.6 + 1.2%, and 100.2 + 0.9%, respectively. For CH4, there were performed 5, 5, 6, and 5 re- covery tests in chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4, respectively, and the average recovery for chamber 1, 2, 3, and 4 were 99.0 + 0.8%, 100.0 + 1.4%, 99.5 + 0.8%, and 99.6 I 0. 8%, respectively.

23: All nutrient flows and subsequent digestibility coefficients in rumen, small intestine, hindgut and total gastro-intestinal tract were calculated as averages from data based on chromic oxide and titanium oxide markers. Dry matter flows at duodenum and ileum, and fecal output of DM were calculated by dividing the daily chromic or titanium oxide input by the chromic or titanium oxide concentration in duodenum, ileum, or feces. Nutrient flows at duodenum and ileum, and fecal output of nutrients were calculated as multiplying the DM flow (average of DM flow based on each marker) by the nutrient concentration in duodenum, ileum, or feces, respectively. Apparent digestibility in each compartment was calculated as the difference between inflow and outflow, divided by inflow, e.g., apparent rumen digestibility was calculated as the differ- ence between nutrient intake and duodenal flow, divided by nutrient intake. Microbial synthesis of DM and CP was calculated from the DM, N, and purine concentration in isolated rumen bacteria, purine con- centration in duodenal DM content, DM flow at the duodenum, and ratio between purine and the given nutrient in the isolated microbial pellet, assuming that all purines flowing at the duodenum originate from rumen microbes (Zinn and Owens, 1986). Microbial efficiency was calculated as microbial CP synthesized divided by true rumen-digested OM, where true rumen-digested OM was calculated as OM intake with feed minus duodenal OM flow (corrected for microbial OM flow at the duodenum; Madsen and Hvelplund, 1988). Average ECM yield for each cow per period was calculated according to Sjaunja et al. (1991).

The ratios of enteric CH4 emisison (L/d) and amount of nutrients digested in rumen or total gastro-intestinal tract (kg/d) were calculated using nutrient digestibility data collected on d 12-16, and feed intake and CH4 emission data on d 16-21, assuming that the digestibility of nutrients and CH4 emission did not change between these dates. The dietary GE supply was calculated according to Volden (2011).

Paired samples t-test was used to compare DMI on digesta sampling week (d 12-16) and CH4 measurement week (d 18-21), and the differ- ence was not found statistically significant (P = 0.29). All experimental data were subjected to analysis of variance using Mixed-procedure of SAS 9.4 version (Statistical Analysis Systems Institute Inc., Cary, NC, USA). The statistical model for analysis of variance was as follows:

Where Y is the dependent variable, H is the overall mean, a is the period of the experiment = 1 to 4), Bj is the effect of diet (j = 100:0. 67:33, 33:67 or 0:100), Wk N(0,02) is the random effect of cow (k = 1 to 4) and N(0,02 is the residual error.
Rumen fermentation characteristics were subjected to analysis of variance for repeated measurements with model as follows: a + Bj + Yh + Byjh Ok + Eijhk

Where Yijhk is the dependent variable, is the overall mean, ai is the period of the experiment (i = 1 to 4), Bj is the effect of diet (j = 100:0. 67:33, 33:67 or 0:100), Yh is the effect of time, Byjh is the interaction between diet and time, Wk ~ N(0,02 is the random effect of cow (k = 1 to 4) and Eijhk N(0,0 is the residual error. The degrees of freedom were calculated according to the Satterthwaite method. The covariance

structure AR(1) was applied for repeated measurements. Results are expressed as least square means for each treatment and standard error of mean (SEM). Significance was declared at P-values 0.05, and a ten- dency was considered at 0.05 < 0.10. Linear (Lin), quadratic (Quad) and cubic (Cub) orthogonal polynomial contrasts were constructed to test the effect of replacing grass-clover silage with maize silage. Loga- rithmic or squared transformations were used for isobutyrate and cap- roate molar proportions to correct for deviations from normality and the homoscedasticity of residuals. For transformed data, least squares means are reported from statistical analysis of untransformed and transformed values, and SEM and P-values from analysis of transformed data.

27: The CF of the main feed ingredients (grass-clover silage, corn silage, rolled wheat, soybean meal and rapeseed meal) in the TMR was calcu- lated using LCA based on the method from Mogensen et al. (2014). In table 7, the input and output of crop production is presented. The functional unit was1 kg DM of feed ready to eat. Average Danish level of fertilizer input and crop yield (Mogensen et al., 2018) were applied and the crops were grown with input of artificial fertilizer as argued in Mogensen et al. (2014). Soybeans are assumed grown in Argentina and Brazil and input, output and processing data are mainly based on Agri-footprint (Zeist et al., 2012). Furthermore, for both grass-clover and maize these calculations were made for a lower and a higher crop yield (+20%) under the assumption that all inputs were the same irre- spective of yield.

The CF of the four experimental feeding strategies was estimated per cow per day. The functional unit for the LCA was '1 kg DMI' and '1 kg ECM milk produced' for Table 8 and 9, respectively. This system does not cover the dry period and production of a heifer for replacement. The resulting CF per kg milk will therefore be lower than the numbers seen for a system including a cow for one year and the heifer for replacement. The cows were assumed to be housed in cubicles and with slatted floor, which is the typical housing system in Denmark. The daily DMI and enteric CH4 emissions used for the LCA were the means from the experiment. Details about the applied LCA method for estimating the environmental impact of feed and cattle production can be found in Mogensen et al. (2014, 2015, 2016). The GHG contribution from carbon (C) change in soil follows the method by Petersen et al. (2013), the in- direct land use change effect (iLUC) was estimated according to Audsley et al. (2009) and the direct land use change effect (dLUC) was estimated according to PAS2050 (BSI, 2008) and Blonk (2013).

29: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. 30: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. A curvilinear pattern was found for the CH4:CO2 ratio with the diet based only on maize silage (0:100) resulting in the

32: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 35: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.
