37: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. A curvilinear pattern was found for the CH4:CO2 ratio with the diet based only on maize silage (0:100) resulting in the

39: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

40: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 42: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 44: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

45: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 47: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 49: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

50: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 3.3. Rumen fermentation and methane production

The rumen fermentation characteristics and CH4 emission are re- ported in Table 6 using data from all 5 experimental animals for all parameters except CH4/ECM, where only 4 animals were included. A significant interaction between diet and sampling time in rumen fermentation characteristics was observed only for glucose (P < 0.01), isobutyrate (P = 0.02), and isovalerate (P = 0.01). The total ruminal VFA concentration was lower for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) than for diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0; P < 0.01), and decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. The same was found for the molar proportions of isobutyrate and valerate (P < 0.01). A curvilinear pattern (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect) was observed with molar proportion of acetate in the rumen, with the proportion being highest in diet 33:67, followed by diet 67:33, and being lowest in the diet based on grass-clover silage (100:0) and the diet based on maize silage (0:100). A curveline pattern was also observed for
butyrate, with the molar proportion being lowest for diet 33:67, inter- mediate for diet 67:33 and 0:100, and highest for 100:0 (P < 0.01 for quadratic effect).

Enteric CH4 emission averaged 560-58 L/d. A tendency for a curvilinear pattern was observed for CH4 emission (L/d) with diet 33:67 tending to result in the highest CH4 emission followed by diet 67:33, and lowest in diets based only on grass-clover silage (100:0) and maize silage (0:100) (P = 0.05 for quadratic effect). Methane emission expressed per kg of DMI decreased linearly (P = 0.02) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, and the emission was 15% lower for the diet entirely based on maize silage compared to the diet based on grass- clover silage. Data from the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the data set for statistical analysis of CH4/ECM, and no effects of diet were observed. The energetic loss as CH4 in proportion of GE intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. 52: <header>3.1. Diet composition, nutrient intake and digestibility </header>

The ingredients and the chemical compositions of the four experi- mental TMRs are listed in Table 1, while the chemical composition of the feedstuffs used in the experiment is listed in Table 2. The diet with maize silage as the only forage (0:100) had higher OM, NDF and starch con- centration, but lower CP concentration than the diet based on grass- clover silage as the only forage (100:0; Table 1). Even though the CP concentration was highest in the 100:0 diet, the amino acids absorbed in the intestine (AAT, planned value), was the lowest for this diet (Table 1). The DMI as well as the nutrient intakes are listed in Table 3 using data from all experimental animals. Dry matter intake increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table 3). The intake of OM (P < 0.01), NDF (P 0.01), ADL (P < 0.01) and starch (P < 0.01) increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. Sugar intake decreased linearly (P < 0.01) with increasing pro- portion of maize silage in the diet.

Overall, diet had only few effects on rumen apparent digestibility of nutrients (Table 4). The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased linearly (P < 0.01) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet (Table The diets had no effect on digestibility of NDF in the rumen. Duodenal flow of microbial DM increased linearly (P = 0.01) with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet.

The apparent small intestinal digestibility of CP tended to increase lineary (P = 0.06) with increased proportion of maize silage in the diet. The hindgut digestibility of CP was also affected by the diets, and a quadric (P < 0.01) and a cubic (P = 0.02) pattern were found, and the digestibility was lowest for the two intermediate diets, diets 67:33 and 33:67. Total tract digestibility of OM, NDF and CP was not affected by the diet (Table 4), whereas total tract digestibility of starch was increased linearly with increased proportion of maize silage.
3.2. Milk production and milk composition

Milk yield and composition are listed in Table 5. The data of the very low yielding late-lactation cow were omitted from the milk production results due to large deviation from the other observations. No linear effects were found for milk or ECM yield. However, significant quadratic and cubic effects were found for milk yield (P 0.05), and tendencies (P = 0.06 and P = 0.08 for quadratic and cubic effects, respectively) were observed for ECM yield (Table 5). No effect on the milk composition was found.

60: In Table 7, the inputs for and outputs of the crop production are presented with the resulting CF per kg DM feedstuff. Calculations were based on Danish average crop yields (Mogensen et al., 2018). The CF of the four diets is listed in Table 8 for each of the three levels of crop yield.

For the average crop yield CF was 10% higher for the maize based diet compared to the grass-clover based diet, when changes in the soil C pool was taken into account. Land use was lowest in the maize diet, due to the higher crop yield of maize (kg DM per ha) compared to grass-clover. In a situation with high crop yield in grass-clover and low yield in maize, CF per kg DM of the maize diet was 55% higher than that of the grass-clover diet. Contrarily, in a situation with a high crop yield in maize and a low yield in grass-clover, CF per kg DM of the diet based on maize became 22% lower than that of the diet based on grass-clover. If iLUC or dLUC were included in CF, the ranking of the diets were the same, irrespective of the crop yields.

In Table 9 CF is shown as a combination of effects from feed, enteric methane, manure, energy input for milk production, with and without effects of changes in the C pool in the soil, iLUC and dLUC. Without taking soil C changes into account, total CF was 16% lower per kg DMI for the diet with the highest amount of maize silage. However, if soil C changes were taken into account, total CF per kg DMI was only 4% lower for the diet with the highest amount of maize silage than for the diet with the lowest amount of maize silage. When iLUC or dLUC were also included, total CF was 6% or 4% lower, respectively, for the maize based diet than for the grass-clover based diet. If CF was expressed per kg ECM (Table 10), there was only minor difference between CF from the diets high in grass-clover (100:0) and high in maize (0:100), respectively, before soil C change was taken into account. When soil C change was taken into account, CF per kg ECM became 12% higher for the diet with the highest amount of maize silage (0:100) compared to the diet with no maize silage. However, for all 3 diets with maize silage CF per kg ECM was almost identical.

The effects of crop yield on each source of CF are shown in Fig. 1. The three crop yield scenarios from Table 9 are illustrated in Fig. 1: A) CF per kg DMI at average crop yield, B) CF per kg DMI at high grass-clover yield and low maize yield and C) CF per kg DMI at low grass-clover yield and high maize yield. In a situation with high crop yield in grass-clover and low yield in maize (Fig. 1B), CF per kg DMI from the diet based on maize silage was 9% higher when including soil C change, and 8% higher when including both changes in soil C and dLUC compared to the diet based on grass-clover silage. Contrarily, in a situation with high crop yield in maize and low yield in grass-clover (Fig. 1C), CF per DMI from the diet based on maize silage was 17% lower when including soil C change than that of the grass-clover silage diet and 12% lower when both soil C change and dLUC was included.

64: 4.1. Composition of feedstuffs and nutrient intake

The DMI of the very low-yielding late-lactation cow did not deviate from other cows used in current experiment. As DMI is the most important factor affecting digesta flow and CH4 emission in dairy cows (Niu et al., 2018), including the low-yielding late-lactation cow in the digestibility and CH4 data sets were considered justified.

The maize silage used in the current experiment with a relatively high starch concentration and low CP concentration represents typical maize silages (e.g. Khan et al., 2015; Heuzé et al., 2017). The optimal DM concentration of maize silage for highest DMI, milk and protein yield has been suggested to be around 30 to 35% (Khan et al., 2015), which is similar to the maize silage used in the current experiment. The grass-clover silage and maize silage used in this experiment were both highly digestible and had equally good ensiling quality based on pH<4, a lactic acid-dominated fermentation pattern, and absence of butyric acid. Thus, the potential negative effects of forage ensiling on animal performance were considered negligible in this experiment.

The gradual substitution of grass-clover silage with maize silage increased DMI, which is a typical response, as also observed in previous research (Phipps et al., 1995, 2005; Hart et al., 2015; Livingstone et al., 2015; van Gastelen et al., 2015). On the other hand, DMI was decreased

in Dewhurst et al. (2001), and unaffected in Brask et al. (2013) when grass-clover silage was completely substituted with maize silage. The response in DMI due to substitution of grass-clover silage with maize silage depends on the maturity of grass-clover silage (Brask et al., 2013) and maize silage (Phipps et al., 1995; Keady et al., 2013). Increased maturity has a negative effect on grass-clover silage intake, and a posi- tive effect on maize silage intake. In grasses the concentration of all fibrous components (NDF, ADF, hemicellulose, cellulose and lignin) typically increases and digestibility of nutrients decreases with maturity (e.g. Rinne et al., 1997), whereas in maize silage the concentration of NDF decreases and that of starch increases with maturity (Phipps et al., 1995; Jensen et al., 2005; Khan et al., 2015).

The interpretation of different experimental results comparing maize silage and grass-clover silage is challenged by the attempts to balance the nutritive composition of these very different forage sources espe- cially in terms of CP, as already pointed out in Dewhurst et al. (2001). The experiments comparing maize silage with grass or grass-clover silage in ruminant nutrition can be roughly classified into two groups based on diet composition. First, into experiments where the nutritive differences of these forages have been fully or partly balanced by changing the proportion or composition of concentrates in the diet (current experiment; Browne et al., 2005), and second, into experiments where only the forage source is changed and the proportion and composition of concentrates stay the same (Brask et al., 2013; van Gastelen et al., 2015). This decision will naturally affect also the con- clusions drawn from these experiments. Indeed, the concentration of CP in the diets decreased with increasing proportion of maize silage in the current experiment, as in Browne et al. (2005), Brask et al. (2013) and van Gastelen et al. (2015), which is notable because diet CP concen- tration can affect DMI and nutrient digestibility (Oldham, 1984). In the current experiment, CP was partly balanced and metabolizable protein (AAT) requirement was fulfilled according to NorFor (Volden 2011). The diets in the current experiment were partly balanced with respect to CP by adjusting the amount of wheat and rapeseed meal in the diets. However, due to higher DMI on maize silage based diets and partly balanced diet composition, the intake of CP did not differ between treatments in the current experiment and it increased in Browne et al. (2005). In contrast, Brask et al. (2013) and van Gastelen et al. (2015) reported decreasing CP intakes with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet. Another challenge rising from diet balancing is different proportions of forage and concentrates between experimental diets. As a result, the proportion of CP originating from forage sources decreased from 68% to 33% when grass-clover silage was gradually substituted with maize silage in the diet in the current experiment. In general, forages have lower metabolizable protein concentration and higher proportion of soluble protein than concentrates (NorFor Feed Tables, 2019). In the current experiment, intake of OM and of starch were increased with increasing proportion of maize silage in the diet, which is in agreement with Browne et al. (2005), Brask et al. (2013) and van Gastelen et al. (2015). Simultaneously, the intake of sugar decreased with increased maize silage proportion in the current experiment as also found by Brask et al. (2013) and van Gastelen et al. (2015). The intake of NDF increased in the current experiment and in Hart et al. (2015), but decreased in van Gastelen et al. (2015) and was unaffected in Brask et al. (2013) when grass or grass-clover silage was substituted with maize silage. The response in NDF intake seems to mainly depend on the maturity of grass-clover silage, which varied largely between afore- mentioned experiments (NDF concentration of 30-52% of DM), whereas the NDF concentration of maize silage has been more similar (36-43% of DM) between experiments. In the current experiment the increase in NDF concentration in the diet with higher proportion of maize was due to the increased amount of rapeseed when maize silage was increased, and this balancing of diets meant that a large part of the NDF (20%) originated from rapeseed meal in the maize silage based diet (0:100), whereas only 3% of the NDF originated from rapeseed meal in the grass-clover based diet (100:0).

67: In all diets in the current experiment, 99% of dietary starch origi- nated from wheat and maize silage. The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased with increasing proportion of maize silage, i.e. when starch intake was increased by up to 3 kg/d and starch from wheat was completely substituted with starch from maize silage in the diet. The same was observed in Brask et al. (2013) for rumen di- gestibility (total tract digestibility was not reported), and in van Gas- telen et al. (2015) for total tract digestibility. In contrast, Browne et al. (2005) reported that total tract digestibility of starch decreased with increasing proportion of maize silage in steer diets. The digestion site of starch is relevant, because the rumen escapable starch that is digested in small intestines can provide more energy to the animal than ruminal digested starch (Owens et al., 1986). The proportion of rumen escapable starch was very low in maize silage-based diets in the current experi- ment, decreasing from 24% of total starch on the 100% grass-clover silage to 9% on 100% maize silage as forage source. This is in agree- ment with previous experiments on maize silage, where the proportion of rumen escapable starch in maize silage-based diets has been 6 to 9% (Jensen et al., 2005; Brask et al., 2013), and 30 to 21% in grass-clover silage-based diets (Brask et al., 2013). In the current experiment, the source of starch differed between the diets. In the predominantly grass-clover silage based diets (100:0 and 67:33) the starch mainly originated from wheat, and in the predominantly maize silage based diets (33:67 and 0:100) the starch originated from maize silage. The ruminal digestibility of starch is greatly influenced by the mechanical processing method and ensiling (Owens et al., 1986; Philippeau and Michalet-Doreau, 1998; Jensen et al., 2005, Moharrery et al., 2014), and therefore the starch source can influence the ruminal digestibility of starch.

Surprisingly, forage source had no effect on rumen and total tract digestibility of NDF in the current experiment, and this contradicts with the results of previous experiments. Browne et al. (2005), Brask et al. (2013), and van Gastelen et al. (2015) all reported lower total tract di- gestibility of NDF for maize silage-based diets than for grass-clover silage-based diets, the same was also true for rumen digestibility of NDF in Brask et al. (2013). However, this may have at least partially been affected by diet balancing, because in experiments without diet balancing the decrease in total tract digestibility of NDF by complete substitution of grass or grass-clover silage with maize silage has been larger, 22% in Brask et al. (2013) and 34% in van Gastelen et al. (2015), than in an experiment with diet balancing, 18% in Browne et al. (2005).
68: In all diets in the current experiment, 99% of dietary starch origi- nated from wheat and maize silage. The apparent ruminal and total tract digestibility of starch increased with increasing proportion of maize silage, i.e. when starch intake was increased by up to 3 kg/d and starch from wheat was completely substituted with starch from maize silage in the diet. The same was observed in Brask et al. (2013) for rumen di- gestibility (total tract digestibility was not reported), and in van Gas- telen et al. (2015) for total tract digestibility. In contrast, Browne et al. (2005) reported that total tract digestibility of starch decreased with increasing proportion of maize silage in steer diets. The digestion site of starch is relevant, because the rumen escapable starch that is digested in small intestines can provide more energy to the animal than ruminal digested starch (Owens et al., 1986). The proportion of rumen escapable starch was very low in maize silage-based diets in the current experi- ment, decreasing from 24% of total starch on the 100% grass-clover silage to 9% on 100% maize silage as forage source. This is in agree- ment with previous experiments on maize silage, where the proportion of rumen escapable starch in maize silage-based diets has been 6 to 9% (Jensen et al., 2005; Brask et al., 2013), and 30 to 21% in grass-clover silage-based diets (Brask et al., 2013). In the current experiment, the source of starch differed between the diets. In the predominantly grass-clover silage based diets (100:0 and 67:33) the starch mainly originated from wheat, and in the predominantly maize silage based diets (33:67 and 0:100) the starch originated from maize silage. The ruminal digestibility of starch is greatly influenced by the mechanical processing method and ensiling (Owens et al., 1986; Philippeau and Michalet-Doreau, 1998; Jensen et al., 2005, Moharrery et al., 2014), and therefore the starch source can influence the ruminal digestibility of starch.

Surprisingly, forage source had no effect on rumen and total tract digestibility of NDF in the current experiment, and this contradicts with the results of previous experiments. Browne et al. (2005), Brask et al. (2013), and van Gastelen et al. (2015) all reported lower total tract di- gestibility of NDF for maize silage-based diets than for grass-clover silage-based diets, the same was also true for rumen digestibility of NDF in Brask et al. (2013). However, this may have at least partially been affected by diet balancing, because in experiments without diet balancing the decrease in total tract digestibility of NDF by complete substitution of grass or grass-clover silage with maize silage has been larger, 22% in Brask et al. (2013) and 34% in van Gastelen et al. (2015), than in an experiment with diet balancing, 18% in Browne et al. (2005).
69: Milk data in a Latin square experiment with a low number of animals must always be evaluated with cation due to a typically large variation between animals. Therefore, also the CF expressed per kg ECM should be interpreted with care. In previous experiments, inclusion of maize silage to dairy cow diets has resulted in higher milk yield than grass silage (Phipps et al., 1995), whereas the substitution of grass or grass-clover silage with maize silage did not affect milk or ECM yield in Brask et al. (2013) and van Gestalen et al. (2015). The highest milk yield in the current experiment was found in the intermediate diets, 67:33 and 33:67.
70: In the current experiment, COWS had ad libitum access to TMR, and generally, DMI is considered as the main variable affecting CH4 emis- sion. Niu et al. (2018) found a significant positive relationship between DMI and CH4 emission demonstrating that as a dairy cow consumes more feed, more CH4 is produced due to greater availability of substrate for microbial fermentation. This was partly confirmed in the current experiment.

In the current experiment, increasing the proportion of maize silage in the diet linearly decreased enteric CH4 in L/kg DMI, which is in agreement with Hart et al. (2015) and van Gastelen et al. (2015). Interestingly, in the current experiment and in van Gastelen et al. (2015) a curvilinear pattern was observed in CH4 emission (L/d), as the inter- mediate diets with 67:33 and 33:67 ratios of grass-clover silage and maize silage, respectively, resulted in the highest CH4 emission in both experiments. The reduction in CH4 emission per kg DMI, when maize silage inclusion was increased, is assumed to be linked to an increase in starch concentration in the maize silage based diets and increased rumen fermentation of starch. Hence, in the 4 diets 1.70, 2.68, 3.95 and 4.69 kg starch per day was fermented in the rumen. These findings agree with the results reported by Hassanat et al. (2013) and van Gastelen et al. (2015). Propionate proportion in the rumen was, however, not affected by diet in the current experiment. Methanogenesis in the rumen is directly associated with the availability of hydrogen (Janssen, 2010). Replacing fiber with starch in the diet promotes formation of propionate and use of hydrogen on the expense of formation of acetate and hydrogen. In the current study, cows fed the two diets with the highest content of maize silage had higher intake of NDF and starch compared to the more grass-clover silage based diets. Aguerre et al. (2011) investi- gated the effect of forage:concentrate ratio and found that cows fed the 47:53 F:C ratio diet, which was the lowest NDF and highest starch diet, emitted 17% less CH4 compared with cows fed the 68:32 F:C ratio diet, which was the highest NDF and lowest starch diet. In the current experiment, the forage ratio was kept similar in the 4 diets, but as mentioned earlier; nutrient composition was confounded with forage source in the current study.

The proportion of GE intake lost as CH4 averaged 6.08% in the current experiment, which is in agreement with the intercontinental average (6.0%) and slightly lower than the European average (6.4%) reported by Niu et al. (2018). Hower, there was a highly significant reduction from 6.73% for the diet with only grass-clover compared to only 5.51% for the diet with only maize.

73: The effect of dairy cattle production on the climate is not merely a result of the enteric CH4 emission. It must be evaluated in a larger perspective, also taking into account the climate effect of producing the feedstuffs used. When comparing grass-clover and maize based diets it is essential also to include changes in the soil C pool. In the current experiment one of the objectives was to use the life cycle perspective, meaning the inclusion of GHG emissions from the whole dairy system from growing the feed crops, enteric CH4 emission from digestion of the diet, and the effect of the manure management, when identifying the diet composition with the lowest overall impact on the climate. When the changes in the soil C pool were disregarded, the CF of producing the feedstuffs (at an average crop yield) for the diet based on maize silage (0:100) was lower compared to the other diets, but higher when changes in the soil C pool were included. This pinpoints that choice of model assumptions can significantly affect the model output and thereby the conclusions and recommendations drawn. The DMI was higher for the maize silage diet, but enteric CH4 per kg DMI was lower in the maize based diet compared to the grass-clover silage diet. These results were calculated assuming average Danish crop yield on the farm.

The findings in the current study confirm the need for using a larger perspective than merely addressing enteric CH4, when a CH4 mitigation strategy based on a change in diet is evaluated. It is essential that the climate impact of producing the diet is also taken into account. The reported CF is highly dependent on the assumptions used. In the present calculations, the average Danish forage yields from conventional, non- organic farms were used. These forage yields will vary from year to year and from farm to farm, as factors such as location, precipitation and soil type will have a significant impact on forage yield. Therefore, this must be taken into consideration when the results are used and

evaluated for a given farm, region, or country. One efficient way to ensure mitigation of CF is to increase the maize and grass-clover yields per hectare. This can be achieved by using better varieties or by improved management without increasing the inputs of fertilizers, pesticides and energy (Vellinga and Hoving, 2011). In the current experiment going from a grass-clover based feeding strategy to a maize silage based feeding strategy reduced CH4 emission. However, a higher inclusion rate of maize in dairy diets will mean ploughing grassland for maize, and therefore lead to momentary losses of soil C. A no-tillage system might decrease the difference between maize and grass-clover in soil C sequestration. Furthermore, not only the choice of forage will have impact on the climate, because also the choice of concentrate feedstuffs needed to balance the differences in the nutrient composition of grass-clover and maize silage within reasonable physiological limits, has an impact on CF.Thus, the complexity of reducing the overall CF is high, and therefore it seems impossible to give a general recommenda- tion regarding the most beneficial ratio of maize and grass-clover silage in the diet as the recommendation highly depends on numerous on-farm factors.

76: The present experiment showed that DMI increased when the dietary proportion of maize silage was increased. Diets had only minor effects on digestibility of nutrients and there was no influence on rumen pH. Total VFA concentration decreased linearly with increased level of maize silage, but there were only minor effects on molar proportions of VFAs in the rumen. However, there was a linear increase in duodenal flow of microbial DM with increasing level of maize silage. Enteric CH4 emission per kg DMI was reduced linearly with increased level of maize silage. Despite the significant reduction in enteric CH4 with increased proportion af maize silage, only a slight decrease in total CF per kg DMI was found, when including CF from feed production, transportation, processing, manure, energy use and C sequestration in the soil, when using a LCA approach. Overall, there is a no clear optimal distribution between maize and grass-clover silage because total CF was highly dependent on crop yield of the forages.
